summary: This notebook provides a baseline pipeline for the MAP competition using a DeBERTa encoder to predict student misconceptions from math Q&A data. It demonstrates how to construct a structured text prompt, engineer a simple correctness feature based on answer popularity, and train a sequence classification model with a custom MAP@3 evaluation metric. The author shows that prompt formatting and leveraging pre-trained LLMs significantly outperform traditional TF-IDF baselines. Readers should learn how to adapt the Hugging Face Trainer for custom metrics and efficiently format top-k predictions for submission.
competition_flow: Loads train and test CSVs, engineers a correctness feature, formats multi-turn Q&A into prompts, tokenizes the data, trains a DeBERTa sequence classifier with a custom MAP@3 metric, and generates a top-3 prediction submission file.
data_reading: Reads train.csv and test.csv with pandas
Fills NaN values in the Misconception column with 'NA'
Uses scikit-learn's LabelEncoder to map the combined Category:MC_Answer string to integer labels
data_processing: Tokenizes text inputs using AutoTokenizer with max_length=256, padding='max_length', and truncation=True
Converts pandas DataFrames to Hugging Face Dataset objects
Applies softmax to model logits to obtain class probabilities
features_engineering: Creates an is_correct feature by identifying the most frequently selected MC_Answer per QuestionId in the training set and merging it back to both train and test dataframes
cv_strategy: Holdout validation split (20% via train_test_split); author mentions 5-KFold as an alternative training strategy in the markdown text.
insights: Modifying the input prompt structure can significantly improve cross-validation scores.
Pre-trained encoder models like DeBERTa outperform traditional TF-IDF approaches for this text classification task.
Answer frequency per question can serve as a useful proxy feature for correctness.
